results.possibleIdentity: Barry Bryant (1940–1997) was a New York-based visual-conceptual artist, filmmaker, and producer active from the late 1960s through the 1990s. He is best known for his pivotal role as the manager and mentor of the pioneering electronic psych-rock band Silver Apples during the late 1960s. Bryant provided the band with a loft studio in New York and encouraged their experimental use of homemade oscillators and electronic equipment, which aligns with the ethos of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He served as the executive producer and wrote liner notes for their debut album, which famously included the instruction 'Play Twice Before Listening.' In 1973, he founded the Samaya Foundation in Manhattan, a non-profit organization dedicated to making Tibetan art and spiritual culture accessible in the West; he later directed the 'Wheel of Time' sand mandala project with Tibetan monks at the American Museum of Natural History in 1988. His work was characterized by interdisciplinary collaboration, such as a 1984 National Endowment for the Arts-funded performance piece with composer Pauline Oliveros and choreographer Lin Lerner. Bryant's artistic legacy is documented in collections such as the Norman Fisher Collection at MOCA Jacksonville and the MoMA Archives.

results.possibleIdentity: Pierre Cordier (1933–2024) was a Belgian artist and photographer known as the "father of the chemigram," a technique he invented in 1956. The chemigram is a cameraless photographic process that combines the physics of painting (using resists like varnish, wax, and oil) with the chemistry of photography (photosensitive emulsion, developer, and fixer) in full light. His work, which is highly experimental and interdisciplinary, aligns with the goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Cordier participated in the E.A.T. exhibition titled "Some More Beginnings" at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968, which showcased collaborations between artists and engineers. He was also a co-founder of the "Generative Fotografie" movement in 1968, which emphasized systematic and process-based image-making. Throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, he explored chromatic research in chemigrams and produced experimental films. His work has been exhibited at major institutions, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York (in the 1967 exhibition "A European Experiment"), the Centre Pompidou in Paris, and the Victoria and Albert Museum in London.

results.possibleIdentity: Norman Peterson (1924–1989) was a prominent Canadian folk artist and woodcarver based in Winnipeg, Manitoba, and later Niagara Falls, Ontario. Active from the mid-1960s through the 1980s, he was best known for his whimsical, hand-carved wooden figurines of animals, particularly his signature owls, birds, and cats. He founded a business in the mid-sixties that focused on making art "affordable to all," utilizing a hybrid of machine work (such as unguarded circular saw blades for feathering patterns) and hand-carving techniques. His works are often signed and are considered collectible mid-century Canadian folk art.

Norman Peterson was a researcher and practitioner involved in community media and communications technology in Canada during the 1970s. He was associated with the Teled Video Services Association and the development of the Media Resources Centre in Nova Scotia. In May 1975, he authored a discussion paper titled "The Community, Communication and Social Change," which reviewed the original assumptions made about community video services. This identity aligns with the technology and social-collaboration goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted the use of new media (like video) for social communication during that period.

Norman Peterson was a musician and educator active at the Eastman School of Music in Rochester, New York, during the late 1950s, 1960s, and 1970s. He served as a secondary organ teacher and is remembered by former students (such as organist Ann Labounsky and Tom Weisflog) for his instruction during that era. While primarily known as a music teacher, the Eastman School was a hub for artistic and musical performance during the period specified.

results.possibleIdentity: Agnes Denes (born May 31, 1931, in Budapest, Hungary) is a pioneering American conceptual, environmental, and ecological artist who rose to international prominence in the late 1960s and 1970s. Raised in Sweden and educated in the United States at the New School and Columbia University, she began her career as a poet before shifting to a practice she calls 'Visual Philosophy,' which integrates mathematics, philosophy, linguistics, and science. She is widely recognized for her early environmental work 'Rice/Tree/Burial' (1968), considered one of the first site-specific ecological performance pieces. Denes was a founding member of the A.I.R. Gallery in 1972, the first artist-run gallery for women in the United States. Her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is documented in historical archives from 1969 and 1970; she submitted proposals for the organization's 'Projects Outside Art' and was featured in technology-focused exhibitions of the era, such as the 1970 'Software' exhibition at the Jewish Museum and the E.A.T.-related 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum. Her major series from this period include the 'Philosophical Drawings' (begun 1969), 'Pyramid Series' (begun 1970), and 'Isometric Systems in Isotropic Space—Map Projections' (begun 1973). She is also well-known for her 1982 public work 'Wheatfield—A Confrontation,' where she planted two acres of wheat in lower Manhattan.

results.possibleIdentity: Nancy Siegel was a prominent administrative and technical figure in the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit established in 1966 to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Active in the late 1960s and 1970s, she served as the Director of Technical Services (or Technical Services Administrator) for the organization. In this capacity, she managed the "Matching Service," a system designed to pair artists with technical professionals for experimental projects. She also contributed to and edited E.A.T. publications, including the newsletter 'Techne', and was involved in the documentation of significant art-technology events such as the 1966 '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering'.

Dr. Nancy J. Siegel is a Professor of Art History at Towson University and a specialist in 19th-century American landscape painting, print culture, and culinary history. While primarily known for her scholarship and curatorial work (such as the 2010 exhibition 'Remember the Ladies: Women of the Hudson River School'), she has been described as a multifaceted figure who merges artistic expression with academic inquiry. She has co-curated exhibitions and published extensively on American art, including the works of Thomas Cole and Susie Barstow. Although her primary academic career spans from the late 1990s to the present, she is associated with historical art research and has held fellowships at institutions like the Smithsonian American Art Museum and the American Antiquarian Society.
